questions.stem: Microbiology is best defined as the scientific study of which subject?
options: Microorganisms, that is organisms too small to be seen clearly with the unaided eye.
The internal organs of large mammals.
Rocks and the minerals that form them.
The motion of planets and stars.
questions.explanation: Microbiology is the branch of biology that studies microorganisms, organisms generally too small to be seen in detail without a microscope. These include bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, and microscopic algae. The prefix micro means small, so the name literally points to the study of small life.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT normally counted among the major groups of microorganisms studied in introductory microbiology?
questions.explanation: The major groups of microorganisms are bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, and microscopic algae. Mammals are large multicellular animals visible to the unaided eye and are not microorganisms. Some microbiology courses also include helminths and prions as topics, but mammals are never included.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A prokaryotic cell is distinguished from a eukaryotic cell mainly by which feature?
options: It has a tail used for swimming.
It lacks a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
It is always larger than a eukaryotic cell.
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells lack a true membrane-bound nucleus, so their genetic material lies free in the cytoplasm in a region called the nucleoid. They also lack membrane-bound organelles such as mitochondria. Eukaryotic cells, by contrast, enclose their DNA in a nucleus and contain such organelles.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Bacteria are classified as which type of cell?
questions.explanation: Bacteria are prokaryotes: single-celled organisms whose DNA is not enclosed in a nucleus. They lack membrane-bound organelles. Fungi, protozoa, and algae are eukaryotic, while viruses are acellular and so fall outside the prokaryote and eukaryote split.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Why are viruses described as acellular rather than as cells?
options: They are not made of cells and cannot reproduce without a host cell.
They have a nucleus but no cytoplasm.
They are larger than all bacteria.
questions.explanation: A virus is essentially genetic material, either DNA or RNA, enclosed in a protein coat, and it is not a cell. Viruses cannot carry out metabolism or reproduce on their own and must hijack the machinery of a living host cell to multiply. For this reason they are called acellular obligate intracellular parasites.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which group of microorganisms is eukaryotic and includes yeasts and moulds?
questions.explanation: Fungi are eukaryotic organisms that include single-celled yeasts and filamentous moulds. They have a true nucleus and a cell wall made largely of chitin. Bacteria and archaea are prokaryotes, and viruses are acellular.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Protozoa are best described as which kind of microorganism?
options: Single-celled eukaryotes, many of which are motile.
questions.explanation: Protozoa are single-celled eukaryotic microorganisms, so they possess a true nucleus. Many are motile, moving by means of pseudopodia, flagella, or cilia. Familiar examples include Amoeba, Paramecium, and the malaria parasite Plasmodium.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: What feature do algae have that distinguishes them from protozoa?
options: They carry out photosynthesis using chlorophyll.
questions.explanation: Algae are eukaryotic microorganisms that contain chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis, making them autotrophic. Protozoa lack chlorophyll and obtain nutrients by ingesting other material, so they are heterotrophic. Both groups are eukaryotic and many live in aquatic habitats.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek is remembered in microbiology chiefly for which achievement?
options: Being among the first to observe microorganisms through simple microscopes he built.
Proposing the germ theory of disease.
questions.explanation: In the seventeenth century Antonie van Leeuwenhoek built simple single-lens microscopes and was among the first to observe and describe living microorganisms, which he called animalcules. His careful drawings of bacteria and protozoa opened the microbial world to study. He did not develop vaccines or antibiotics.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: The germ theory of disease, supported by the work of Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch, states which idea?
options: Diseases arise spontaneously from non-living matter.
Many diseases are caused by microorganisms.
All microorganisms are harmful to humans.
Diseases are caused only by poisonous vapours.
questions.explanation: The germ theory holds that many diseases are caused by microorganisms that invade and multiply in a host. The work of Pasteur and Koch in the nineteenth century established this view, replacing older ideas such as the miasma, or bad-air, theory. The theory does not claim that all microbes are harmful, since most are harmless or beneficial.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Pasteur's swan-neck flask experiments are best known for refuting which idea?
questions.explanation: Spontaneous generation was the belief that living organisms could arise directly from non-living matter. Pasteur showed that sterile broth in a swan-neck flask remained free of microbial growth as long as airborne microbes could not reach it, demonstrating that microbes come from pre-existing microbes. His experiments thereby refuted spontaneous generation.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Koch's postulates are a set of criteria used to establish which relationship?
options: That two species compete for the same food.
That a population has reached carrying capacity.
That a specific microorganism causes a specific disease.
questions.explanation: Koch's postulates are logical criteria for proving that a particular microorganism is the cause of a particular infectious disease. They guide the experimental steps of isolating the suspected agent and reproducing the disease. They concern causation of disease, not ecology or cell structure.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: According to Koch's postulates, what must be true of the suspected pathogen in diseased hosts?
options: It must be found in all hosts with the disease but not in healthy hosts.
It must be unable to grow in pure culture.
It must be a virus rather than a bacterium.
It must be absent from every diseased host.
questions.explanation: The first of Koch's postulates requires that the microorganism be present in every case of the disease and absent from healthy individuals. Later postulates require isolating it in pure culture, reproducing the disease by inoculating a healthy host, and re-isolating the same organism. Together these steps tie one organism to one disease.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which order lists the four steps of Koch's postulates correctly?
options: Inoculate a host, re-isolate the microbe, find it in diseased hosts, grow it in culture.
Find the microbe in all diseased hosts, grow it in pure culture, inoculate a healthy host to cause disease, re-isolate the same microbe.
Grow it in culture, destroy it, observe the host recover, publish the result.
Re-isolate the microbe, inoculate a host, grow it in culture, find it in diseased hosts.
questions.explanation: Koch's postulates proceed in a fixed logical order. First the microbe is found in all diseased hosts and absent from healthy ones. It is then grown in pure culture, used to inoculate a healthy host that develops the disease, and finally re-isolated from that host and shown to match the original. This closed loop establishes causation.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which statement about the unit used to measure most bacteria is correct?
options: Bacteria are typically a few kilometres in size.
Bacteria are typically a few centimetres in size.
Bacteria are typically a few micrometres in size.
Bacteria are typically a few metres in size.
questions.explanation: Most bacteria are only a few micrometres long, where one micrometre is one millionth of a metre. A typical rod-shaped bacterium might be around two micrometres in length, far below the limit of unaided human vision. This small size is why microscopy is essential in microbiology.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which instrument is essential for observing the detailed structure of bacteria?
options: A spectrometer for measuring colour only.
questions.explanation: Because bacteria are only a few micrometres across, they cannot be resolved by the unaided eye and require a microscope. Light microscopes reveal bacterial shape and arrangement, while electron microscopes resolve finer detail and are needed to see most viruses. The telescope and barometer serve unrelated purposes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which type of microscope is generally required to see the structure of most viruses?
questions.explanation: Most viruses are far smaller than bacteria, often only tens of nanometres across, which is below the resolving power of light microscopes. The electron microscope, which uses beams of electrons instead of light, provides the much higher resolution needed to image them. Light microscopes and hand lenses cannot resolve such small particles.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly pairs a microbial group with its cell type?
questions.explanation: Protozoa are single-celled eukaryotes, so the pairing of protozoa with eukaryotic is correct. Bacteria are prokaryotic, not eukaryotic, while fungi and algae are eukaryotic rather than prokaryotic or acellular. Keeping these pairings straight is a basic goal of introductory microbiology.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Most microorganisms in nature are best described as which of the following?
options: Harmful pathogens that cause disease in humans.
Harmless or beneficial, with only a minority causing disease.
Unable to survive outside the human body.
questions.explanation: Although microbiology often emphasises disease, the great majority of microorganisms are harmless or actively beneficial. They drive nutrient cycling, food production, and the breakdown of wastes, and many live harmlessly in and on the human body. Only a small minority, the pathogens, cause disease.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: A microorganism that causes disease is called by which term?
questions.explanation: A pathogen is any microorganism capable of causing disease in a host. A host is the organism that harbours the pathogen, while a vector is an agent, often an insect, that transmits a pathogen from one host to another. A saprophyte feeds on dead organic matter and is usually not a pathogen.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which scientist is most associated with the discovery of penicillin, an early antibiotic produced by a mould?
questions.explanation: Alexander Fleming is credited with the discovery of penicillin in 1928, after noticing that a Penicillium mould inhibited the growth of nearby bacteria. This finding launched the antibiotic era. Van Leeuwenhoek pioneered microscopy, while Mendel and Darwin worked in genetics and evolution rather than microbiology.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures why microbiology matters to fields such as medicine and public health?
options: Microorganisms have no effect on human health.
Microorganisms cause many infectious diseases and also underpin food production, sanitation, and biotechnology.
Microorganisms are studied only for historical interest.
questions.explanation: Microbiology underpins medicine and public health because microorganisms cause infectious diseases that must be diagnosed, treated, and prevented. The same organisms are central to food and drink production, sanitation, and biotechnology such as the manufacture of antibiotics and vaccines. Microbes are found throughout the natural environment, not only in laboratories.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Which statement about the genetic material of viruses is correct?
options: A virus stores its genes in a nucleus.
A virus always contains both DNA and RNA.
A virus contains either DNA or RNA, but not usually both.
A virus contains no genetic material at all.
questions.explanation: A virus particle carries a single type of nucleic acid as its genome, either DNA or RNA, but not normally both at once. This nucleic acid is enclosed in a protein coat called a capsid. Because viruses are acellular, they have no nucleus in which to keep their genes.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Where is the genetic material located in a typical prokaryotic cell?
options: Attached to the outside of the cell wall.
Free in the cytoplasm, in a region called the nucleoid.
questions.explanation: Prokaryotes lack a membrane-bound nucleus, so their single circular chromosome lies free in the cytoplasm in an irregular region called the nucleoid. They also have no mitochondria. This arrangement is one of the defining contrasts between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Classify each microorganism by cell type or structure, choosing the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: Bacteria are {{1}} cells, meaning they have no membrane-bound nucleus. Fungi and protozoa are {{2}} cells, meaning they do have a true nucleus. A virus is {{3}}, since it is not made of cells at all.
questions.explanation: Bacteria are prokaryotes and lack a membrane-bound nucleus. Fungi and protozoa are eukaryotes with a true nucleus. A virus is acellular, being genetic material in a protein coat rather than a cell.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Fill in the names of the pioneers and ideas in the history of microbiology.
questions.solution: The scientist who first observed microorganisms through simple microscopes, calling them animalcules, was {{1}}. The idea that living things can arise from non-living matter, refuted by Pasteur, is called {{2}} generation. The criteria used to prove that a specific microbe causes a specific disease are known as the postulates of {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek first observed microorganisms with simple microscopes and named them animalcules. Spontaneous generation was the disproved idea that life arises from non-living matter. Koch's postulates are the criteria linking a microbe to a disease.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Match each microbial group to its defining trait by selecting the correct option.
questions.solution: Algae differ from protozoa because algae carry out {{1}} using chlorophyll. The group made up of yeasts and moulds is the {{2}}. A microorganism that cannot reproduce on its own and must use a host cell is a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Algae contain chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis, unlike the heterotrophic protozoa. Fungi is the group that contains yeasts and moulds. A virus is an obligate intracellular parasite that must use a host cell to reproduce.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)

questions.stem: Complete the description of the scale and tools of microbiology by choosing the correct option for each blank.
questions.solution: Most bacteria are only a few {{1}} in size, so they cannot be seen clearly with the unaided eye. To observe most viruses, which are even smaller, a scientist needs an {{2}} microscope. In a prokaryotic cell the genetic material lies free in the cytoplasm in a region called the {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Bacteria are typically a few micrometres long. Most viruses are too small for light microscopy and require an electron microscope. In prokaryotes the chromosome sits in the cytoplasm in a region called the nucleoid, since there is no true nucleus.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Biology for Mathematics course (BIOL 181)
